findings.title: Any authenticated user can escalate to system_admin by PATCHing their own user_profiles row (account & tenant takeover)
findings.problem: The `own profile update` policy is `create policy "own profile update" on public.user_profiles for update using ((id = auth.uid()));` (schema.sql:1740-1742) with NO WITH CHECK clause. Postgres defaults a missing UPDATE WITH CHECK to the USING expression, which constrains only `id` — the privileged columns `role` (schema.sql:820) and `company_id` (819) remain freely writable in the new row. All four exploit legs are present: (1) no WITH CHECK on the update policy; (2) `grant select, insert, update, delete on all tables in schema public to authenticated;` gives every logged-in user table-wide UPDATE (_archive/20260618_grant_table_privileges.sql:24); (3) no BEFORE UPDATE trigger exists on user_profiles — the TRIGGERS block (schema.sql:1358-1371) only touches public.tickets and auth.users; (4) app_role() (1164-1172) and app_is_company_wide() (1144-1152) read `role` directly from user_profiles, and proxy.ts route gating keys off the same role, so mutating the column immediately confers privilege. This defeats the handle_new_user() signup clamp (1261-1289) that forces new signups to 'individual'.
findings.whyItMatters: A self-signed-up 'individual' (or any store_manager/supplier) can call PATCH /rest/v1/user_profiles?id=eq.<their-uid> with the public anon apikey + their own bearer JWT and body {"role":"system_admin"} — optionally also {"company_id":"<victim-company-uuid>"} — and instantly become a platform/tenant super-admin. That unlocks /admin/* (infra dashboards), company-wide read/write across nearly every table (app_is_company_wide()), and full cross-tenant data access if company_id is switched. This is a complete authorization bypass and multi-tenant breach reachable by anyone who can register.
findings.evidence: schema.sql:1741 `create policy "own profile update" on public.user_profiles for update using ((id = auth.uid()));` (no with check). grant file:24 `grant select, insert, update, delete on all tables in schema public to authenticated;`. app_role() schema.sql:1170 `select role from public.user_profiles where id = auth.uid();`.
findings.rootCause: UPDATE policy relies on the implicit WITH CHECK=USING, but USING pins only the row identity (id), not the mutable privileged columns, so column-level tampering passes.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: A non-privileged user must be unable to change their own role or company_id; role/tenant changes may only occur via trusted service-role paths.
findings.fixApproach: Add an explicit WITH CHECK to the update policy that freezes privileged columns, e.g. WITH CHECK (id = auth.uid() AND role = (select role from public.user_profiles where id = auth.uid()) AND company_id IS NOT DISTINCT FROM (select company_id from public.user_profiles where id = auth.uid())); OR, more robustly, add a BEFORE UPDATE trigger on user_profiles that rejects any change to role/company_id unless the session is service_role, and narrow the authenticated UPDATE grant to a column list excluding role/company_id. Claude can write the migration + fold into schema.sql (CLAUDE); OWNER must apply it to live AND immediately audit `select id,email,role,company_id from user_profiles where role in ('system_admin','executive')` for any already-escalated accounts.
testsRequired: As a freshly signed-up individual, PATCH own user_profiles role->system_admin via PostgREST and assert 403/no-op
Assert store_manager cannot change own company_id
Assert service-role/onboarding paths can still set privileged roles
findings.scoreImpact: Blocks go-live; single most severe issue in the RLS surface.

findings.title: tickets UPDATE RLS policy lets an assigned supplier self-complete their own ticket (workflow + separation-of-duties bypass)
findings.lines: schema.sql:1728-1730; app_supplier_ids schema.sql:1184-1192; user-client INSERT evidence app/api/tickets/route.ts:85
findings.problem: Any supplier assigned to a ticket (supplier_id in their app_supplier_ids) can issue a direct PostgREST call, e.g. PATCH {SUPABASE_URL}/rest/v1/tickets?id=eq.<id> with {"status":"completed","completed_at":"...","closed_out_by":...}, and RLS will allow it (USING matches on the old row; with no WITH CHECK the new row only needs supplier_id/company_id unchanged). They can also overwrite quote_value, priority, needs_review, and clear current_blocker/sla_paused/*_due_at fields.
findings.whyItMatters: This defeats the entire workflow state machine and the core separation of duties: the supplier who performs the work can mark it 'completed' with zero RM sign-off, skip quoting/approval, hide SLA breaches, and alter the financial quote_value on the record. It is exploitable without the app UI using only the anon URL + the supplier's own session token, both present in the browser.
findings.evidence: Policy: create policy "tickets update" on public.tickets for update using ((company_id = app_company_id()) AND (app_is_company_wide() OR region_id IN (app_region_ids()) OR supplier_id IN (app_supplier_ids()))) — NO WITH CHECK. app_supplier_ids() returns the caller's linked supplier_ids (schema.sql:1184-1192). A supplier's browser session is an 'authenticated' Postgres role subject to RLS; the app already performs ticket INSERT via the user-scoped (RLS-bound) client at app/api/tickets/route.ts:85, proving 'authenticated' has table privileges and RLS is the live guard. No code anywhere updates tickets via the user client (all ticket writes use createAdminClient()), so this UPDATE policy is never exercised legitimately.
findings.rootCause: RLS treated as secondary to route-level authZ, but the policy is still reachable by the browser JWT; the UPDATE policy grants far more than any legitimate browser path needs and omits WITH CHECK / column scoping.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Suppliers (and any non-service-role client) must not be able to change tickets.status or other lifecycle/financial columns directly; all ticket mutations must flow through the server routes that use the workflow engine.
findings.fixApproach: Since no user-scoped client updates tickets, drop the browser UPDATE policy entirely (or restrict USING to app_is_company_wide() only) so the service-role admin client remains the sole writer; if any browser UPDATE is ever needed, add a WITH CHECK that pins status/quote_value/completed_at to their old values (or a trigger that forbids status changes outside SECURITY DEFINER). Author as a migration for the human to apply to live.
testsRequired: As a supplier session, attempt PATCH tickets?id=eq.<assigned> set status=completed → must be rejected
As a supplier, attempt to change quote_value → rejected
Regression: normal supplier ticket reads + realtime still work (SELECT policy untouched)
findings.scoreImpact: Critical production blocker — direct authorization bypass of the completion/sign-off control.

findings.title: GET /api/suppliers returns every tenant's supplier directory (missing company_id filter)
findings.problem: requireAdmin() selects only `role` (line 26) and checks `profile?.role !== 'supplier'` (line 27) — it does NOT read or require company_id. GET then runs `createAdminClient().from('suppliers').select('*').order('company_name')` (lines 36-39) with NO `.eq('company_id', ...)` filter. Because createAdminClient() is the service-role client that bypasses RLS, any supplier-role user (any tenant) receives the full cross-tenant suppliers table.
findings.whyItMatters: Leaks PII and commercial data of every client company's subcontractor directory — contact_name, email, phone, address, vat_number, qualification_number, notes (schema.sql:539-549) — to unrelated tenants. Direct multi-tenant confidentiality breach; the route-level authZ is the only guard once the admin client is used, and it is absent.
findings.evidence: route.ts:26 `.select('role').eq('id', user.id).single()`; :27 `if (profile?.role !== 'supplier') return null`; :36-39 `adminClient.from('suppliers').select('*').order('company_name')`. Contrast [id]/route.ts:29-30 which selects `role, company_id` and requires `profile.company_id`, and :69/:89 which scope mutations with `.eq('company_id', ctx.companyId)`. Schema RLS confirms company scoping: schema.sql:1663-1664 `suppliers read ... using ((company_id = app_company_id()) AND (...))`.
findings.rootCause: suppliers table is company-scoped (FK company_id, RLS, [id] routes) but the collection GET treats it as a global directory and uses the RLS-bypassing admin client without re-applying the tenant filter.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: A supplier-role user may only read suppliers where company_id equals their own profile.company_id.
findings.fixApproach: Make requireAdmin() select `role, company_id` and return the companyId (as [id]/route.ts already does); in GET add `.eq('company_id', ctx.companyId)`. Consider adding a rateLimit to GET as defense-in-depth. Optionally enforce a DB-side company_id NOT NULL so the admin path can't silently drop the tenant tag.
testsRequired: Supplier user in company A calls GET /api/suppliers and receives only company A's suppliers (company B rows absent)
Row with NULL company_id is not returned to any tenant
findings.scoreImpact: Cross-tenant data exposure — treat as launch blocker for multi-tenant confidentiality.

findings.title: Store manager (client) can tamper with supplier quote amount/status — quotes UPDATE has no WITH CHECK
findings.problem: `create policy "quotes update" on public.quotes for update using (((company_id = app_company_id()) AND app_can_see_ticket(ticket_id)));` (schema.sql:1524-1526) has no WITH CHECK, so it defaults to the USING expression. app_can_see_ticket() (1196-1209) returns true for store users whose store owns the ticket (t.store_id IN app_store_ids()). Because `authenticated` holds table-wide UPDATE, a store_manager can PATCH the supplier's quote row — including `amount`, `amount_incl_vat`, `status`, `decline_reason` (schema.sql:223-229) — directly via PostgREST.
findings.whyItMatters: The client party can silently rewrite the contractor's quoted price or flip a quote's status (e.g. force 'accepted'/'declined'), corrupting the core quoting/financial workflow and any downstream reporting. It is a financial-integrity and repudiation issue between the two parties of a job.
findings.evidence: schema.sql:1525-1526 quotes update policy (USING only); 1202-1207 app_can_see_ticket includes `t.store_id in (select public.app_store_ids())`; quotes.amount at 223.
findings.rootCause: Visibility (can_see_ticket, which includes the client store) is being used as the write gate; no role restriction to the owning supplier, and no WITH CHECK to constrain mutated columns.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Only the awarded/owning supplier (or an admin) may modify a quote; the client store may read and accept/decline via a controlled path, not free-form column writes.
findings.fixApproach: Restrict the quotes UPDATE policy to the supplier who owns the quote (supplier_id IN app_supplier_ids()) or admin roles, and add an explicit WITH CHECK; route client accept/decline through a server API that only flips status. Also tighten `quotes write` (INSERT, 1528-1530) so only suppliers/admins can insert quotes, not any ticket-visible store user.
testsRequired: As store_manager on own ticket, PATCH quotes.amount -> expect denied
As owning supplier, PATCH own quote.amount -> allowed
As store_manager, INSERT a quote row -> denied

findings.title: GET /api/suppliers leaks every tenant's supplier directory (no company_id filter, RLS bypassed)
findings.problem: GET /api/suppliers authenticates only on role: requireAdmin() checks profile.role==='supplier' and returns the user WITHOUT capturing company_id (lines 22-29). The handler then runs `adminClient.from('suppliers').select('*').order('company_name')` with NO `.eq('company_id', ...)` filter (lines 36-39) on the service-role client, which bypasses RLS. So ANY supplier-role user — including an unverified self-signup 'pool' supplier still pending admin review — can read the full suppliers row for EVERY company on the platform: company_name, contact_name, email, phone, address, trade, VAT number, qualification_number, qualification_expiry, and notes. Every other route that touches this table is correctly tenant-scoped (suppliers/[id]/route.ts PATCH/DELETE use `.eq('company_id', ctx.companyId)`; regional/suppliers/[id]/route.ts checks `sup.company_id !== companyId`; provision.add_supplier sets company_id), which confirms `suppliers` is meant to be company-scoped and this route is the inconsistency.
findings.whyItMatters: Multi-tenant isolation is the core security property of the app (per CLAUDE.md, route-level authZ is the only guard when the admin/service-role client bypasses RLS). This is a direct cross-tenant disclosure of B2B PII and commercially sensitive vendor data (contact details, VAT numbers, qualification records) across all tenants, readable by the lowest-trust authenticated principal (a just-registered, not-yet-verified supplier). The app/supplier/suppliers/page.tsx UI was removed in v3 (it now just `redirect('/supplier')`) and no client calls this endpoint, so it is dead-from-the-UI but still a live, reachable HTTP endpoint.
findings.evidence: app/api/suppliers/route.ts:36-39  `const { data, error } = await adminClient.from('suppliers').select('*').order('company_name')` — no company_id predicate. requireAdmin() (lines 26-28) returns `user` on `profile?.role === 'supplier'` with no company_id.
findings.rootCause: v2 endpoint left live after the v3 model made `suppliers` company-scoped; the GET was never updated to filter by the caller's company_id and requireAdmin() never captured it.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: A supplier-role user can only read suppliers rows belonging to their own company (and, if intended, the Motiv pool) — never another tenant's directory.
findings.fixApproach: Either delete the dead route file (the UI page already redirects away) or scope it: capture company_id in requireAdmin() and add `.eq('company_id', companyId)` to the GET. Given suppliers[].company_id can be NULL for Motiv-pool rows, decide explicitly whether pool suppliers should be visible.
testsRequired: Supplier A (company X) GETs /api/suppliers and receives only company X's suppliers
Self-signup pending-review supplier (company_id null) cannot read any company's suppliers
findings.scoreImpact: Confirmed cross-tenant PII disclosure — production blocker for go-live.

findings.title: signoffs (FOR ALL), quotes (UPDATE) and ticket_variations (FOR ALL) writable directly by see-ticket users — forged completions & quote-amount tampering
findings.lines: signoffs schema.sql:1585-1588; quotes update schema.sql:1524-1526; ticket_variations schema.sql:1702-1705; app_can_see_ticket schema.sql:1196-1209
findings.problem: A store_manager (client) — or a same-company supplier — can, via PostgREST: INSERT a signoff row with status='accepted' or UPDATE/DELETE the real one (forge or destroy completion certificates); UPDATE quotes.amount/status on their tickets (change the agreed price after submission); and INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE ticket_variations with no role check (e.g. set a variation status='approved'). None of these have a role or workflow-state guard — only company/visibility scoping.
findings.whyItMatters: Financial integrity (quote amounts), evidence integrity (completion certificates/proof), and variation-order approvals can all be manipulated directly, bypassing the review flow. The app writes all of these exclusively via the service-role admin client, so — like the tickets policy — these browser-reachable write policies serve no legitimate purpose.
findings.evidence: signoffs: create policy "signoffs write" ... for all using ((company_id = app_company_id()) AND app_can_see_ticket(ticket_id)) with check (company_id = app_company_id()). quotes: create policy "quotes update" ... for update using ((company_id = app_company_id()) AND app_can_see_ticket(ticket_id)) (no WITH CHECK). ticket_variations: for all using/with check (company_id = app_company_id()) only. app_can_see_ticket() is true for a store_manager linked to the ticket's store, an RM of its region, or the assigned supplier.
findings.rootCause: Write policies scoped only by tenant/visibility (app_can_see_ticket / company_id) with no role or state constraints, while the app relies solely on route-level authZ.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: quotes, signoffs and ticket_variations must not be insertable/updatable/deletable by store managers or suppliers via direct DB access; only the server routes (admin client) should write them.
findings.fixApproach: Drop the browser INSERT/UPDATE/ALL write policies on quotes, signoffs and ticket_variations (keep the SELECT policies), leaving the service-role admin client as sole writer; or add strict WITH CHECK/role predicates. Ship as a migration.
testsRequired: Store-manager session: UPDATE quotes.amount on own ticket → rejected
Supplier session: INSERT signoff status=accepted → rejected
Store-manager: UPDATE ticket_variations status=approved → rejected
findings.scoreImpact: High — record and financial tampering surface.

findings.title: Losing/closed competitor supplier keeps write access to another supplier's awarded ticket (hasAccess ignores invite status)
findings.component: Ticket state-machine transition — supplier access gate
findings.problem: hasAccess() for role 'supplier' returns true if EITHER ticket.supplier_id is one of the caller's supplier companies OR any ticket_suppliers row exists for the caller on this ticket. The ticket_suppliers lookup (line 360: `.select('id').eq('ticket_id', ticket.id).in('supplier_id', mine).maybeSingle()`) does not filter by status, so an invite row left as 'closed'/'declined' after another supplier is awarded still grants access. In the competitive-quote model, when an RM approves a quote the other invitees' rows are set to 'closed' (quote-decision route line 142) rather than deleted — so every losing supplier retains a row and therefore passes hasAccess even though the ticket is awarded to a different company.
findings.whyItMatters: A supplier who lost the bid can still drive the awarded supplier's ticket through the state machine: resolveTransition only checks role='supplier'+status, so once the job is in_progress a losing supplier can call start_work, submit_completion (inserts a signoff), submit_variation (injects a bogus variation-order financial doc), or schedule. All side-effect rows are stamped supplier_id = ticket.supplier_id (the AWARDED supplier), so the tampering is attributed to a company that did not perform it, and the ticket moves state out from under the real supplier and RM. This is broken access control across supplier tenants.
findings.rootCause: Access check uses invite existence, not active-invite status; award closes invites instead of removing them, and the gate doesn't distinguish 'awarded/active' from 'closed/declined'.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Only the awarded supplier (ticket.supplier_id) may perform post-award execution actions; invited-but-not-awarded suppliers may act only during the commercial/quoting phase and only via quoting endpoints.
findings.fixApproach: In hasAccess(), gate the ticket_suppliers fallback on status IN ('invited','quoted') AND restrict it to commercial-phase statuses; for execution/close-out statuses require mine.includes(ticket.supplier_id). Alternatively resolve access per-action (quoting actions allow active invitees; execution actions require the awarded supplier).
testsRequired: Supplier B invited then ticket awarded to A; B calls transition submit_completion on in_progress → expect 403
Supplier B (active invite) submits quote during quote_requested → still allowed
Awarded supplier A submit_completion → allowed
findings.scoreImpact: Broken object-level authorization between competing supplier tenants on shared tickets.

findings.title: supplierId / supplierIds written to ticket without validating the supplier belongs to the ticket's company or region (cross-tenant assignment)
findings.problem: On transition actions validate / require_assessment / request_quote / assign_snag the handler writes `updates.supplier_id = body.supplierId` (and inserts ticket_quote_requests / snag rows) using the raw client-supplied UUID with no check that the supplier exists or is in-scope for the ticket's company/region. The /assign route likewise inserts ticket_suppliers invites for every UUID in body.supplierIds (filtered only to be strings) without verifying each is a company-owned or pool supplier. RLS is bypassed (admin client), so there is no DB-level backstop.
findings.whyItMatters: An RM/executive (or anyone who can reach a role-permitted action) can attach an arbitrary supplier UUID — including another company's private supplier — to a ticket. That foreign supplier's users then satisfy hasAccess()/resolveViewer() and receive notifications, a push, and read/write access to the ticket (description, photos, chat, signoff docs), leaking one tenant's maintenance data to another tenant's contractor. It also corrupts the supplier directory relationship and audit trail.
findings.rootCause: Missing allowlist check that the supplier id is selectable by this ticket's company/region before persisting it or creating an invite.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: A ticket may only be assigned to, or invited to quote, suppliers that are visible to the ticket's company (company-owned suppliers or shared Motiv-pool suppliers).
findings.fixApproach: Before writing supplier_id / inserting ticket_suppliers, look up each supplier id and assert it is company-scoped to ticket.company_id or a shared pool supplier; reject unknown/out-of-scope ids with 400/403.
testsRequired: assign with a supplier UUID from another company → expect 403/400, no ticket_suppliers row, no notification
transition validate with foreign supplierId → rejected
findings.scoreImpact: Cross-tenant data exposure via unvalidated supplier assignment.

findings.title: POST /api/suppliers creates supplier rows with NULL company_id (no tenant tag on write)
findings.problem: The insert (lines 67-79) sets company_name/contact/email/etc. but never sets company_id, so every supplier created via this route gets company_id = NULL. requireAdmin() (lines 22-29) doesn't even fetch company_id, so the caller's tenant is unknown to the handler.
findings.whyItMatters: NULL-tenant rows are globally visible through the unfiltered GET (Finding 1) yet cannot be edited or deleted through the company-scoped /api/suppliers/[id] routes (`.eq('company_id', ctx.companyId)` never matches NULL), leaving orphaned, unmanageable records. It also silently defeats the intended per-company isolation of the directory. No privilege escalation into the Motiv pool: is_motiv defaults false and the body schema can't set it.
findings.evidence: route.ts:64-81 `adminClient.from('suppliers').insert({ company_name..., notes... })` — no company_id key. requireAdmin :26 selects only `role`. suppliers.company_id is nullable (schema.sql:537).
findings.rootCause: Same as Finding 1 — collection route doesn't carry the caller's company_id; write path was never tenant-tagged.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: New supplier rows must be stamped with the creating user's company_id.
findings.fixApproach: Have requireAdmin() return companyId and add `company_id: ctx.companyId` to the insert (mirroring provision's add_supplier at provision/route.ts:189 which correctly sets company_id).
testsRequired: Supplier in company A creates a supplier; row has company_id = A
Created supplier is subsequently editable/deletable via /api/suppliers/[id] by company A

findings.title: POST /api/suppliers/bulk imports up to 500 suppliers with NULL company_id
findings.problem: The row mapper (lines 34-46) builds each insert object from CSV fields but never adds company_id; the batch insert (line 52) therefore writes up to 500 rows with company_id = NULL. Auth is only session + `role === 'supplier'` (lines 14-18) with no company_id resolved.
findings.whyItMatters: Same tenant-orphaning as Finding 2 but at bulk scale — a single upload can create hundreds of globally-visible (via Finding 1), unmanageable supplier records. Amplifies the cross-tenant surface.
findings.evidence: bulk/route.ts:34-46 mapped object omits company_id; :52 `adminClient.from('suppliers').insert(rows).select()`.
findings.rootCause: Bulk route inherits the untenanted-write pattern of the collection routes.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Every bulk-imported row must carry the uploader's company_id.
findings.fixApproach: Resolve the caller's company_id (select it in the auth check) and set `company_id` on each mapped row before insert.
testsRequired: Bulk import by company A produces rows all stamped company_id = A

findings.title: Lower-privilege company members can forge approvals / decision_items / signoffs / snags — FOR ALL policies weaken WITH CHECK to company_id only
findings.problem: Four FOR ALL write policies use a rich USING (role/region/visibility gate) but a WITH CHECK of only `company_id = app_company_id()`: approvals write (1462-1465), decisions write (1489-1491), signoffs write (1586-1588), snags write (1610-1612). Since INSERT is evaluated against WITH CHECK alone, any company member with the table INSERT grant (store_manager, supplier, etc.) can insert these rows regardless of the role/region/can_see_ticket restriction that governs reading and updating; UPDATE new-rows are likewise only re-checked for company_id, allowing a row to be retargeted to a ticket/region the actor otherwise cannot see.
findings.whyItMatters: These tables represent trusted governance artifacts — RM approvals, executive decision items, completion sign-offs, and snag (defect) records. A low-privilege user forging or retargeting them corrupts the audit/governance trail and can, e.g., fabricate a completion sign-off or an approval within their tenant.
findings.evidence: e.g. snags: schema.sql:1610-1612 USING `... AND (app_is_company_wide() OR (app_role() = 'regional_manager'))` but `with check ((company_id = app_company_id()))`; signoffs 1586-1588 USING includes app_can_see_ticket but WITH CHECK drops it.
findings.rootCause: WITH CHECK was written narrower than USING, so the authorization predicate that guards reads/updates is absent on the INSERT and new-row paths.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: INSERT and UPDATE-new-row must enforce the same role/region/visibility predicate as the USING clause.
findings.fixApproach: Make each WITH CHECK mirror its USING predicate (company + role/region/can_see_ticket). One migration updating all four policies; fold into schema.sql.
testsRequired: As store_manager, INSERT into snags/approvals/signoffs/decision_items -> denied
As RM, UPDATE an approval to reference a ticket outside my region -> denied

findings.title: No role gate on supplier_escalations / supplier_invites / ticket_variations — any company member can CRUD; invite tokens exposed company-wide
findings.problem: Three FOR ALL policies gate on tenant only, with no role check: supplier_escalations admin (1632-1634), supplier_invites admin (1641-1643), ticket_variations admin (1703-1705) — all `using/with check (company_id = app_company_id())`. Any authenticated company member (store_manager, supplier) can insert/update/delete these rows. For supplier_invites, the paired `supplier_invites read` (1645-1647) is also company_id-only, so the secret `token` column (schema.sql:485) is readable by every member of the company.
findings.whyItMatters: A supplier could delete escalations raised against itself or fabricate cost variations; any member can read/mint supplier invite tokens, enabling onboarding hijack or forged invites within the tenant. These are privileged operations that should be limited to admin/regional roles.
findings.evidence: schema.sql:1632-1634 supplier_escalations admin (no role); 1641-1643 supplier_invites admin (no role); token column schema.sql:485 `token text not null`; 1703-1705 ticket_variations admin (no role).
findings.rootCause: Policies defined with tenant isolation but the role predicate was omitted.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Writes limited to appropriate admin roles; invite tokens not readable by non-admins.
findings.fixApproach: Add `AND app_role() = ANY(ARRAY['system_admin','regional_manager', ...])` to USING and WITH CHECK on all three write policies, and scope supplier_invites read to admin roles (or drop token from client-visible reads). One migration; fold into schema.sql.
testsRequired: As supplier, DELETE a supplier_escalation -> denied
As store_manager, SELECT supplier_invites.token -> denied
As store_manager, INSERT ticket_variations -> denied

findings.title: ticket_updates insert does not bind author_id/author_role to the caller — comment/audit impersonation
findings.problem: `create policy "ticket_updates write" on public.ticket_updates for insert with check (app_can_see_ticket(ticket_id));` (schema.sql:1698-1700) checks only ticket visibility. The author columns (author_id references user_profiles, author_role) are not constrained to auth.uid()/the caller's actual role, so any user who can see the ticket can insert an update attributed to another user or a higher role.
findings.whyItMatters: ticket_updates is the ticket comment/activity trail. Spoofing author_id/author_role lets a user fabricate messages appearing to come from the RM, supplier, or admin — a repudiation / social-engineering vector inside the tenant.
findings.evidence: schema.sql:1699-1700 with check is app_can_see_ticket(ticket_id) only; author_id FK at schema.sql:1034.
findings.rootCause: WITH CHECK omits an author_id = auth.uid() binding.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Inserted updates must be attributed to the authenticated caller.
findings.fixApproach: Add `AND author_id = auth.uid()` to the WITH CHECK (and validate author_role server-side, or drop it in favor of deriving role from the profile). Same treatment worth applying to ticket_evidence.uploaded_by (schema.sql:1674-1676).
testsRequired: Insert a ticket_update with author_id != auth.uid() -> denied

findings.title: app_settings is gated by role only, with no tenant scope — cross-tenant admin overwrite
findings.problem: `create policy "app_settings admin" on public.app_settings for all using (public.app_role() = 'system_admin') with check (public.app_role() = 'system_admin');` (schema.sql:2012-2015). The table has no company_id column (single-row-per-key global blob, schema.sql:2004-2008), so the policy cannot scope by tenant. A system_admin of ANY company can read/overwrite the global app settings (app name, branding, colours) that affect every tenant.
findings.whyItMatters: Harmless in the current single-company deployment, but if the platform ever onboards a second company with its own system_admin, that admin could alter global branding/config for all tenants. It also means app_settings is not multi-tenant even though the rest of the schema is.
findings.evidence: schema.sql:2004-2008 table has only (key, value, updated_at); 2013-2015 role-only policy.
findings.rootCause: Global (non-tenant) design of app_settings combined with a per-company system_admin role model.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Global platform settings mutable only by the true platform owner, not by any tenant's system_admin.
findings.fixApproach: Decide the model: either keep app_settings platform-global and restrict writes to a dedicated platform-owner (a specific user id / a super-admin flag) rather than any system_admin, or add company_id and make settings per-tenant. Since all reads/writes already go through the service-role admin routes, the direct-table policy is defence-in-depth — tighten it accordingly.
testsRequired: As system_admin of company B, UPDATE app_settings -> denied (once model chosen)

findings.title: POST /api/suppliers and /api/suppliers/bulk create suppliers with no company_id (untenanted writes)
findings.problem: Both write handlers gate only on role==='supplier' and insert into `suppliers` via the service-role client WITHOUT setting company_id — the insert object (suppliers/route.ts lines 67-79; bulk lines 34-46) omits company_id entirely, so rows are created with company_id = NULL. requireAdmin() in suppliers/route.ts doesn't capture company_id at all. bulk accepts up to 500 rows per call. Contrast provision.add_supplier and supplier onboard, which always set company_id (or deliberately NULL for standalone pool suppliers).
findings.whyItMatters: NULL-company supplier rows are un-owned: they can never be edited/deleted via suppliers/[id]/route.ts (which requires `company_id = ctx.companyId`), and — combined with the unfiltered GET in finding #1 — they are globally readable. This both pollutes the tenant data model and provides a write vector into a table other tenants can read. It is the write half of the same isolation defect.
findings.evidence: app/api/suppliers/route.ts:67-79 insert object has no company_id key; app/api/suppliers/bulk/route.ts:34-46 same. requireAdmin() (lines 22-29) never reads company_id.
findings.rootCause: Same v2/v3 divergence as finding #1 — writes predate the company_id scoping model.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Any supplier row created by a supplier-role user is stamped with that user's company_id and is invisible to other tenants.
findings.fixApproach: Delete these dead routes, or capture the caller's company_id in requireAdmin() and stamp `company_id: companyId` on every inserted row (rejecting supplier-role users with a null company_id).
testsRequired: POST /api/suppliers as company X supplier → row has company_id = X
bulk import rows all carry the caller's company_id

findings.title: Ticket supplier assignment/invite never validates the supplier_id belongs to the ticket's company (or Motiv pool)
findings.problem: Several handlers copy a client-supplied supplier id straight onto the ticket / into ticket_suppliers with no check that the id refers to a supplier in the ticket's own company directory (or a Motiv-pool supplier). In transition/route.ts: `validate` (`updates.supplier_id = body.supplierId`, line 130), `require_assessment` (145), `request_quote` (152-153), and `assign_snag` (261-263). In assign/route.ts the `supplierIds[]` from the body are inserted into ticket_suppliers (lines 78-81) after only string-filtering (line 30). regional/tickets/route.ts does the same (lines 79-82). The caller IS authenticated and ownership of the TICKET is verified (company match + rmOwnsTicket / invite), but the SUPPLIER reference itself is unvalidated. Per lib/workflow.ts, `validate` is RM/exec only, while `request_quote`/`require_assessment` are also allowed to the supplier role.
findings.whyItMatters: A curious or malicious RM (or, via request_quote/require_assessment, a supplier already on the ticket) can point supplier_id at — or invite — a suppliers row owned by a DIFFERENT company. hasAccess() in the transition route then grants that foreign supplier's users access to the ticket (they can view job title/description/photos and submit a quote), because access is gated purely on the supplier_users → supplier_id link. That is a cross-tenant exposure of one ticket's data. Exploitation requires knowing a valid foreign supplier UUID (not enumerable), so this is a missing-control / architectural risk rather than a trivially exploitable IDOR, but the server should not trust the client for the tenant scope of a foreign key it writes.
findings.evidence: transition/route.ts:130 `if (body.supplierId) updates.supplier_id = body.supplierId` — no lookup of suppliers.company_id vs ticket.company_id. assign/route.ts:78-81 inserts `fresh.map(supplier_id => ({ company_id: ticket.company_id, ..., supplier_id }))` for arbitrary body supplier_ids.
findings.rootCause: Assignment routes trust the UI supplier picker to only offer in-company/pool suppliers and never re-validate server-side.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: A ticket can only be assigned to, or have quote invites created for, suppliers in its own company's directory or the approved Motiv pool.
findings.fixApproach: Before writing supplier_id / inserting ticket_suppliers, load the suppliers row(s) and assert `company_id === ticket.company_id` OR the row is a verified Motiv-pool supplier (is_motiv/company_id null per the pool rules). Reject otherwise. Apply the same guard in transition (validate/require_assessment/request_quote/assign_snag), assign, and regional/tickets.
testsRequired: RM of company X cannot assign/invite a supplier_id owned solely by company Y
supplier-role user cannot use request_quote to set supplier_id to an out-of-scope supplier

findings.title: transition-engine submit_quote accepts Infinity / over-cap amounts (weaker than parseAmount)
findings.problem: A supplier posting action:'submit_quote' to /transition can store a quote amount of Infinity or an absurd value into quotes.amount and tickets.quote_value, bypassing the R100M sanity ceiling enforced on the parallel route.
findings.whyItMatters: Corrupts financial figures and any downstream SLA/health/report aggregation that sums quote_value; inconsistent validation across the two quote-submission paths.
findings.evidence: case 'submit_quote': const amount = Number(body.amount); if (!amount || amount <= 0) return 400 ... quotes.insert({ amount }); updates.quote_value = amount. Number('Infinity') → Infinity passes (!Infinity is false, Infinity<=0 is false); 999_999_999_999 also passes. The dedicated /submit-quote route instead uses parseAmount() (lib/api-error.ts) which rejects non-finite, <=0 and > MAX_MONEY_AMOUNT (R100,000,000).
findings.rootCause: Two quote-submission code paths with divergent amount validation; the engine path never adopted the hardened parseAmount.
findings.fixApproach: Replace the inline Number()/>0 check in transition submit_quote with parseAmount() and return the same 400 on null.
testsRequired: POST /transition submit_quote amount=Infinity → 400
amount=200000000 (over cap) → 400

findings.title: transition approve_quote diverges from /quote-decision: leaves invites open, never sets supplier_id, marks ALL pending quotes accepted
findings.problem: approve_quote is still a live endpoint (roles regional_manager/executive/individual). If invoked, the ticket reaches 'accepted' with supplier_id=null and its ticket_suppliers invites still 'invited'/'quoted'. A different invited supplier can then POST /submit-quote (their invite isn't closed), which resets the ticket back to status 'quoted' — a workflow reversal from an accepted state — and multiple quotes end up status='accepted', corrupting the award record.
findings.whyItMatters: Two parallel award implementations, one incomplete, allow an accepted ticket to be reversed and the award/financial state to become inconsistent.
findings.evidence: transition approve_quote: updates.quote_decision_status='approved'; admin.from('quotes').update({status:'accepted'}).eq('ticket_id',id).eq('status','pending') — updates EVERY pending quote, does NOT set tickets.supplier_id, and does NOT close ticket_suppliers invites. The real UI path (RmTicketActions.tsx:740) uses /quote-decision, which awards one supplier, declines other pending quotes, and closes/awards the ticket_suppliers rows. submit-quote only blocks invite status in ('awarded','closed','declined').
findings.rootCause: Legacy single-supplier approve_quote transition never reconciled with the competitive /quote-decision model.
findings.fixApproach: Either remove approve_quote from lib/workflow + the transition switch, or make it award a single quote and close the other ticket_suppliers invites exactly like /quote-decision; and add a source-status guard to /submit-quote so quotes can't be submitted once the ticket has left the commercial phase.
testsRequired: Call approve_quote then have a second invited supplier submit-quote → must not reset status to quoted
approve_quote must not leave >1 accepted quote

findings.title: tickets.assigned_user_id, asset_id, technician_id have no foreign keys
findings.problem: Three ticket columns that clearly reference other tables carry no referential integrity. A ticket can point at an asset_id / technician_id / assigned_user_id that never existed or was deleted, and deleting an asset/technician/user leaves dangling ticket references with no RESTRICT and no index.
findings.whyItMatters: Dispatch and asset-history features join tickets to technicians/assets; orphaned UUIDs surface as broken joins, blank names, or query errors, and the DB provides no guard that a technician assigned to a ticket even belongs to the same company/supplier. Because these are also unindexed, filters/joins on them seq-scan.
findings.evidence: Columns declared uuid at schema.sql:730/733/805 but the tickets FK block (schema.sql:1045-1052) adds FKs only for company_id, edited_by, repeat_defect_group_id, closed_out_by, created_by, supplier_id, region_id, store_id. assigned_user_id (→user_profiles), asset_id (→assets), technician_id (→technicians) are absent. Confirmed via grep: only asset_health_scores/asset_service_history/pm_plans reference assets(id); no `tickets add foreign key (asset_id|technician_id|assigned_user_id)`.
findings.rootCause: Columns were added by later migrations (technician_id, dispatch) without a matching FK constraint.
findings.fixApproach: Add FKs in a migration: tickets.asset_id→assets(id), tickets.technician_id→technicians(id), tickets.assigned_user_id→user_profiles(id), each ON DELETE SET NULL (nullable cols), plus a supporting index on each; verify no existing orphan rows first (SELECT ... WHERE asset_id NOT IN (SELECT id FROM assets)).

findings.title: ratings table missing FKs on company_id, supplier_id, contractor_id, rated_by
findings.problem: The ratings table stores who was rated (supplier_id/contractor_id) and who rated (rated_by) and the tenant (company_id) with no referential integrity on any of them except ticket_id.
findings.whyItMatters: Supplier performance scoring aggregates ratings by supplier_id; an orphaned or cross-tenant supplier_id silently corrupts a supplier's score, and rated_by can point at a deleted profile. No index on supplier_id/company_id also means the aggregation seq-scans.
findings.evidence: schema.sql:957 is the only ratings FK: `ratings add foreign key (ticket_id) references tickets(id)`. Columns company_id (238), supplier_id (240), contractor_id (241), rated_by (242) have no FK. So a rating can reference a non-existent supplier or rater.
findings.fixApproach: Add FKs ratings.company_id→companies(id), ratings.supplier_id→suppliers(id), ratings.rated_by→user_profiles(id); decide contractor_id's referent (technicians?) and add FK or drop the column; add indexes on supplier_id and company_id. Backfill/clean orphans first.

findings.title: technicians table has zero foreign keys (company_id, supplier_id unenforced)
findings.problem: A technician row's company_id and supplier_id are free-form UUIDs — nothing enforces that a technician belongs to a real supplier or company, and nothing cascades/restricts when a supplier is removed.
findings.whyItMatters: This is a multi-tenant table; with RLS bypassed by the service-role client, route-level authZ is the only guard, and the DB adds no backstop that a technician_id assigned to a ticket is scoped to the same supplier/company. Deleting a supplier orphans its technicians silently.
findings.evidence: technicians declared with company_id (560) and supplier_id (561) but there is no `alter table public.technicians add foreign key ...` anywhere in schema.sql:924-1055 (grep confirmed no match). The table is fully unconstrained referentially.
findings.fixApproach: Add technicians.supplier_id→suppliers(id) ON DELETE CASCADE and technicians.company_id→companies(id); index both. Clean orphans first.

findings.title: No CHECK constraints on enum-like text columns (status/priority/type/severity/author_role) — typos persist silently
findings.problem: State machines (ticket.status, quote.status, signoff.status, snag.status, whatsapp_sessions.status), ratings.score, and role tags like ticket_chat_messages.author_role are unconstrained text/int. A mistyped or code-drifted value writes successfully and corrupts filters, RAG scoring, and realtime consumers.
findings.whyItMatters: The health engine and RLS branch on exact status strings; a silent bad value (or an out-of-range ratings.score) skews health scores, hides tickets from dashboards, or breaks the sign-off flow, with no DB error to catch it. ticket_chat_messages.author_role has only a code comment ('regional_manager' | 'supplier') as its contract.
findings.evidence: grep for `add constraint`/`check (` returns only RLS `WITH CHECK` policies — zero table CHECK constraints in schema.sql. Every state column is bare `text ... default '...'`. That these are truly free-form is corroborated by app code: app/api/tickets/[id]/quotes/route.ts:59 lists status values ('info_requested','assigned','assessment','quote_requested','quoted','quote_revision','suppliers_declined') far beyond the documented open→quoted→accepted→in_progress→pending_sign_off→completed set, with nothing constraining them.
findings.rootCause: Schema built additively via information_schema reconstruction; CHECKs were never modelled (see header disclaimer).
findings.fixApproach: Because the live value set is broad and drifts, prefer adding CHECK constraints from the authoritative app enums (or reference lookup tables) rather than a narrow list; at minimum add ratings.score CHECK (score BETWEEN 1 AND 5) and author_role CHECK (author_role IN ('regional_manager','supplier')). Enumerate live DISTINCT values before constraining status columns to avoid rejecting valid rows.

findings.title: FK / hot-path indexes not present in schema.sql and likely absent (tickets by company_id/store_id/region_id/supplier_id/status, quotes.ticket_id, notifications(user_id,read))
findings.problem: Every RLS-scoped ticket query filters company_id plus region_id/store_id/supplier_id/status; if those indexes were never added by a migration, they seq-scan the tickets table, and quote lookups seq-scan quotes by ticket_id.
findings.whyItMatters: On the multi-tenant hot path (dashboards, queues, realtime refresh) missing indexes degrade linearly with row count and inflate DB CPU on the free tier. This must be verified against live, not assumed present.
findings.evidence: The only explicit tickets index is tickets_created_by_idx (schema.sql:1346). No index on company_id/store_id/region_id/supplier_id/status is declared. quotes has no ticket_id index declared, yet the quotes read policy (1517) and every quote fetch filter on ticket_id. notifications has notifications_user_archived_idx (user_id, archived_at, created_at) (1356) but not (user_id, read) used by unread counts. Postgres does NOT auto-index FK columns. The schema header (schema.sql:9-12) states secondary indexes are NOT captured, so live cannot be confirmed from this file.
findings.fixApproach: OWNER: run `select indexname, indexdef from pg_indexes where schemaname='public' and tablename in ('tickets','quotes','notifications')` on live and paste back. CLAUDE: add any missing btree indexes — tickets(company_id), tickets(store_id), tickets(region_id), tickets(supplier_id), tickets(status), quotes(ticket_id), notifications(user_id, read) — and fold them into schema.sql.

findings.title: FKs are almost entirely NO ACTION; user-account erasure chain is effectively blocked
findings.lines: 1044 (notifications.ticket_id set null), 1043 (ticket_events cascade), 1054 (user_profiles→auth.users cascade), 945/955/1049 (children of user_profiles NO ACTION)
findings.problem: Deleting an auth user cascades to their user_profiles row, which is then blocked by every NO ACTION child (tickets they created, their notifications, quotes they submitted, etc.), so the whole delete aborts. Likewise a ticket, store, supplier, or company cannot be hard-deleted while any child row exists.
findings.whyItMatters: The RESTRICT-heavy design is good for preventing orphans, but it means account/data erasure (POPIA/GDPR 'right to be forgotten', relevant for this SA-market app) cannot be done with a plain delete and there is no cascade/anonymisation path. Operators may hit opaque FK-violation errors when trying to remove test/duplicate entities.
findings.evidence: Only a handful of FKs specify ON DELETE (push_subscriptions/user_profiles→auth.users cascade at 951/1054; supplier_sla_acceptances/verification_docs cascade at 1005/1006; ticket_events cascade at 1043; notifications.ticket_id set null at 1044; projects.* cascade). All other FKs default to NO ACTION. user_profiles.id→auth.users is ON DELETE CASCADE (1054), but user_profiles is referenced NO ACTION by tickets.created_by (1049), notifications.user_id (945), quotes.submitted_by (955), and many more.
findings.fixApproach: Decide the deletion policy explicitly: keep soft-delete (active/closed_at) as the norm and document that hard-delete is unsupported, OR add an erasure routine that anonymises user_profiles-referencing rows (set actor/created_by to a tombstone) before delete. Set audit/actor FKs to ON DELETE SET NULL so history survives user removal.

findings.title: WhatsApp webhook writes caller PII (phone numbers + free-text ticket content) to server logs
findings.problem: Despite the comment at line 514 ("Do NOT log the full payload — it contains the sender's phone number and message text (PII)"), the handler then logs exactly that: line 523 `console.log(... from: ${message.from})` logs the sender's raw WhatsApp phone number; line 644 logs the normalised phone (`phone: "${normalisedPhone}"`); line 626 `console.log('[WhatsApp] Extracted:', extracted)` logs the full extracted object including the free-text `title`/`description` transcribed from the store manager's voice note/message (which can contain names, locations, incident detail). These go to Vercel function logs and to any future log drain.
findings.whyItMatters: This is an SA app subject to POPIA. Personal identifiers (mobile numbers) and user-authored content in plaintext logs are a privacy exposure: anyone with Vercel log access (or a drain destination) sees them, and they linger beyond the intended data flow. The code's own comment shows the intent was to avoid this.
findings.evidence: L523 `console.log(`[WhatsApp] Message received — type: ${message.type}, from: ${message.from}`)`; L644 `console.log(`[WhatsApp] Profile lookup — phone: "${normalisedPhone}", role: ...`)`; L626 `console.log(`[WhatsApp] Extracted:`, extracted)`
findings.rootCause: Debug logging left in place that contradicts the module's stated no-PII logging policy.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: No raw phone numbers or user message content are emitted to application logs.
findings.fixApproach: Drop the phone number and `extracted` content from these logs; log only non-identifying shape (message.type, a hashed/last-4 phone token, session id). Keep the existing safe log at L519.

findings.title: /view omits tenant (company) match and has no rate limit — cross-company writes into ticket_views audit table
findings.problem: The handler checks only that the caller has a company_id and that the ticket exists; it never compares ticket.company_id to the caller's company_id. It then upserts a ticket_views row using company_id: ticket.company_id (the TARGET ticket's company) with the caller as viewer. There is also no rateLimit() call on this write endpoint.
findings.whyItMatters: Any authenticated company user can forge audit 'view' rows against tickets belonging to other companies, polluting the cross-tenant audit trail (who-viewed-what) that RMs/execs rely on, and can do so unthrottled. Impact is limited to integrity of the ticket_views audit data (no data is returned, no ticket state changes), hence low, but it is a genuine missing tenant guard on a service-role (RLS-bypassing) write.
findings.rootCause: Tenant compare and rate limit both omitted on a low-value write endpoint.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Only a user who legitimately has access to a ticket may record a view on it, and the write is rate-limited.
findings.fixApproach: After loading the ticket, reject when ticket.company_id !== prof.company_id (or reuse the ticket-access helper), and add a rateLimit('view:{user.id}',…) guard.
testsRequired: Company-A user POSTs /view on a company-B ticket → 403/404, no ticket_views row

findings.title: /seen omits tenant match and has no rate limit — cross-company writes into ticket_reads
findings.problem: Checks role/company existence and ticket existence but not ticket.company_id === caller.company_id, then upserts ticket_reads with the target ticket's company_id and the caller's user_id. No rateLimit() on this write.
findings.whyItMatters: Any authenticated user (including pool suppliers/individuals with no company) can create/update a read-state row scoped to another company's ticket, mildly polluting cross-tenant read-tracking. No data is returned and no ticket state changes, so impact is low — but it is another RLS-bypassing write missing the tenant guard.
findings.rootCause: Same pattern as /view: tenant compare and throttle omitted.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Only a user with legitimate access to the ticket may bump their read watermark on it, throttled.
findings.fixApproach: Add a ticket-access/company check before the upsert and a rateLimit guard.
testsRequired: User with no access POSTs /seen on a foreign ticket → 403/404, no ticket_reads row

findings.title: Supplier self-signup onboarding reveals whether an email is already registered (user enumeration)
findings.problem: Before creating the account, the self-signup path queries user_profiles by email and returns a distinct 'An account already exists for this email — please log in.' message when a match exists, allowing an attacker to probe which email addresses have Motiv accounts. The endpoint's only throttle is a coarse global 20/60s limit (line 76), not per-target.
findings.whyItMatters: Account-existence oracle aids targeted phishing/credential-stuffing. Lower impact than the suppliers leak and partly a UX tradeoff, but it diverges from the app's own no-enumeration stance in forgot-password.
findings.evidence: onboard/route.ts:142 `admin.from('user_profiles').select('id').ilike('email', email).maybeSingle()`; :143 `if (existing) return bad('An account already exists for this email — please log in.')`. Compare forgot-password/route.ts:19,29 which always returns {ok:true} to avoid this.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Signup should not confirm account existence pre-authentication (rely on the createUser uniqueness error, already handled at lines 159-163), or gate the pre-check behind a per-email rate limit.
findings.fixApproach: Drop the friendly pre-check (createUser already returns a generic 'account already exists' at 159-163) or return an identical generic message for both branches; optionally add a per-email rate limit.

findings.title: supplier/assign-rm clear-path deletes ALL regional_user links for the store's region
findings.problem: When regionalManagerId is empty (clear assignment), the handler deletes every regional_users row for the store's region (`.delete().eq('region_id', store.region_id)`), not just the link related to this store/RM. Because stores link to RMs through the shared region, clearing one store's RM unassigns every RM from that entire region.
findings.whyItMatters: Tenant-safe (region is checked to belong to the caller's company at line 41), but an operator intending to clear one store's RM can silently strip RM coverage for all stores in that region, causing missed notifications/SLA ownership. Data-integrity/availability footgun rather than a confidentiality bug.
findings.evidence: assign-rm/route.ts:58-61 `adminClient.from('regional_users').delete().eq('region_id', store.region_id)`. The code comment at :33-36 acknowledges the region-scoped link, but the delete is unconditional across the region.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Clearing a store's RM should only remove the intended RM's link, or the UI/semantics should make the region-wide effect explicit and guarded.
findings.fixApproach: Scope the delete to the specific RM (add `.eq('user_id', <currentRmId>)`) or redesign the store↔RM assignment to not mutate region-wide regional_users; at minimum confirm the region-wide intent.

findings.title: Private-bucket storage upload policies are unscoped (bucket + logged-in only)
findings.problem: All upload policies are `with check (bucket_id = '<x>' AND auth.uid() IS NOT NULL)` (schema.sql:1773-1787 for the five ticket/quote/supplier/completion buckets; 1975-1977 for project-files). There is no per-user or per-company path prefix constraint, so any authenticated user could write arbitrary objects to any private bucket, and (for project-files) upload despite the table being system_admin-write-only. The schema comment (1757-1763) documents these as inert because storage RLS does not receive the user's JWT on this migrated project (browser uploads 403 and go through the service-role /api/uploads route).
findings.whyItMatters: Currently non-exploitable per the documented JWT limitation, but the policy is the sole DB-level guard if that limitation is ever fixed (or on a fresh project that inherits JWT claims), at which point it permits cross-user storage abuse and cost. It is a latent config weakness, not a live breach.
findings.evidence: schema.sql:1774-1775 completion-docs upload; 1782-1784 ticket-photos upload; 1976-1977 project-files upload; comment 1757-1763.
findings.rootCause: Upload policies check only presence of a session, not ownership of the target path.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: If storage RLS is ever the live write path, uploads must be constrained to a per-user/company path prefix and (for project-files) to admin roles.
findings.fixApproach: Add a name/path prefix predicate (e.g. `(storage.foldername(name))[1] = auth.uid()::text`) and role checks where appropriate, or explicitly document that these are intentionally inert and keep /api/uploads as the enforced path.
testsRequired: If storage RLS active: upload to another user's folder -> denied

findings.title: schedule action lets a supplier set technician_id to any technician UUID (no roster ownership check)
findings.problem: In the `schedule` transition, `if (body.technicianId !== undefined) updates.technician_id = body.technicianId || null` (line 195) writes a client-supplied technician id onto the ticket without verifying the technician belongs to the acting supplier's roster (technicians.supplier_id ∈ the caller's supplier_ids). Every other technician mutation (supplier/technicians/[id]/route.ts) correctly checks `ctx.supplierIds.includes(tech.supplier_id)`.
findings.whyItMatters: A supplier could stamp another supplier's technician onto a ticket they control, corrupting the attend/dispatch record and cross-referencing a technician row outside their tenant. Low impact (no direct data disclosure; technician rows carry name+phone) but it is an unvalidated cross-supplier foreign-key write.
findings.evidence: transition/route.ts:195 — direct assignment of body.technicianId with no `technicians` lookup, unlike supplier/technicians/[id]/route.ts:23-24 which enforces roster ownership.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: technician_id can only be set to a technician on the acting supplier's own roster.
findings.fixApproach: When technicianId is provided, look up the technician and confirm its supplier_id is one of the acting supplier's supplier_ids before writing; otherwise reject.
testsRequired: supplier cannot schedule with a technicianId belonging to a different supplier

findings.title: Supplier add_update flips ticket status open→in_progress outside the workflow engine
findings.problem: Posting a text update directly sets a ticket's status to in_progress if it is 'open', bypassing lib/workflow entirely (skips assign→quote→approve→accept→start_work). Currently latent because fresh 'open' tickets have supplier_id=null (tickets.route.ts:88) and the route gate requires a non-null matching supplier_id, so it is presently unreachable — but any future path that sets supplier_id on an open ticket (import, WhatsApp, manual) turns it into a live state-machine bypass.
findings.whyItMatters: A non-engine status write is a latent integrity hole; the same file's own header comment states all status changes must go through /transition.
findings.evidence: case 'add_update': ... await admin.from('tickets').update({ last_supplier_update_at: now, status: ticket.status === 'open' ? 'in_progress' : ticket.status }).eq('id', ticketId). Access gate at :40 requires ticket.supplier_id to be in the caller's supplier_ids.
findings.rootCause: Leftover shortcut from the earlier open→in_progress lifecycle.
findings.fixApproach: Remove the inline status flip; add_update should only touch last_supplier_update_at and the update row.
testsRequired: add_update must never change tickets.status

findings.title: ticket_suppliers has no unique(ticket_id,supplier_id); assign route doesn't de-duplicate supplierIds (duplicate supplier assignment)
findings.problem: Duplicate invites can be created for one supplier on one ticket (via a repeated id in the payload or concurrent assigns), and there is no DB constraint to prevent it.
findings.whyItMatters: Weakens invite/award integrity and can double-count a supplier in quote panels/notifications; downstream code mostly dedups by supplier so impact is limited, but the missing constraint is a real gap.
findings.evidence: ticket_suppliers PK is (id) only; no unique constraint on (ticket_id, supplier_id). assign: supplierIds = body.supplierIds.filter(string) with no de-dup; fresh = supplierIds.filter(!existing) then insert(fresh.map(...)). A repeated supplierId in the request array lands in `fresh` twice → two invite rows for the same supplier on the same ticket.
findings.rootCause: No uniqueness constraint + no input de-duplication.
findings.fixApproach: Add unique(ticket_id, supplier_id) to ticket_suppliers (idempotent migration) and de-dup supplierIds in the assign route.
testsRequired: assign with [X,X] creates a single invite
concurrent assigns don't create duplicate rows

findings.title: Evidence can be uploaded after close-out (no workflow-state gate on add_evidence / ticket_evidence)
findings.problem: A supplier can keep inserting completion evidence and toggling the ticket's *_uploaded flags on a terminal (completed) ticket.
findings.whyItMatters: Post-approval evidence mutation pollutes the ticket record after the RM has signed off. Impact is bounded because the immutable per-round snapshot lives in the `signoffs` row (which is what the RM reviewed), so the sign-off decision itself isn't retroactively altered — hence low severity.
findings.evidence: case 'add_evidence' checks only that the caller's supplier owns the ticket (:40) — no ticket.status check — then inserts ticket_evidence and flips before/after/coc/invoice *_uploaded flags. After close_out, tickets.supplier_id is retained, so the awarded supplier still passes the gate on a 'completed' ticket. The RLS "ticket_evidence write" policy is likewise insert-with-check app_can_see_ticket only, no state guard.
findings.rootCause: No terminal-status guard on evidence writes.
findings.fixApproach: Reject add_evidence when ticket.status is terminal (completed/cancelled/declined); optionally add the same guard in the ticket_evidence RLS check.
testsRequired: add_evidence on a completed ticket → rejected

findings.title: Additional child columns without FKs (ticket_suppliers.quote_id/company_id, daily_briefings.company_id, supplier_sla_acceptances.user_id, supplier_verification_docs.uploaded_by, ticket_events.company_id, store_ticket_counters.store_id)
findings.problem: A scattering of reference columns lack FKs, so they can hold stale/invalid UUIDs and never block or cascade on parent deletion.
findings.whyItMatters: Individually low-impact (mostly audit/counter/link tables), but collectively they erode the invariant that a UUID column always points at a live row, which downstream joins assume. ticket_suppliers.quote_id in particular links an invite to its quote with no guarantee the quote exists.
findings.evidence: ticket_suppliers FKs (schema.sql:1031-1032) cover only ticket_id/supplier_id — quote_id (662) and company_id (657) unconstrained. daily_briefings has no FK in the whole FK block. supplier_sla_acceptances adds only supplier_id (1005) — user_id (514) has none. supplier_verification_docs adds only supplier_id (1006) — uploaded_by (529) has none. ticket_events adds only ticket_id (1043) — company_id (712) has none. store_ticket_counters.store_id (435) has no FK.
findings.fixApproach: Add the corresponding FKs (quote_id→quotes, company_id→companies, user_id/uploaded_by→user_profiles, store_id→stores). Keep uploaded_by/user_id ON DELETE SET NULL where the audit row should survive the user.

findings.title: Multi-write workflows are non-atomic (ticket insert + notification fan-out, project milestone + file) — partial writes possible
findings.problem: Workflows that must be all-or-nothing at the domain level (ticket + region-manager notification; project store milestone timestamp + its evidence file; status change + child insert) are executed as independent commits, so a mid-sequence failure leaves inconsistent state.
findings.whyItMatters: For notifications a lost message is tolerable, but for status-change + audit/child-row sequences a partial write can desync the ticket state machine from its evidence, and there is no automatic recovery. This is inherent to using the JS client per-statement rather than a Postgres function.
findings.evidence: In the reference route the ticket is inserted and committed at app/api/tickets/route.ts:85, then a separate notifications insert runs at line 98 with no enclosing transaction (Supabase JS issues each as its own statement/commit). If the notifications insert throws, the ticket already exists with no notification; conversely other flows update ticket status then insert child rows without rollback. The DB offers no transaction wrapper here.
findings.fixApproach: For sequences that must be atomic, move the multi-write into a single SECURITY DEFINER Postgres function (RPC) so it runs in one transaction; for best-effort fan-out (notifications), keep sequential but wrap in try/catch and never let a notification failure surface as a ticket-create error. Audit which routes chain a status/ticket write with a dependent child insert.

findings.title: schema.sql explicitly does not capture CHECK constraints or secondary indexes — integrity review of those is unverifiable against live
findings.problem: The 'single source of truth' file cannot confirm or deny whether live has the CHECK constraints and FK/hot-path indexes flagged above. Findings about those are therefore best-effort until reconciled with a real dump.
findings.whyItMatters: It creates a standing blind spot: schema.sql can look clean while live is missing (or has extra) constraints/indexes, and vice-versa. Any go-live DB sign-off should be against pg_dump, not this file.
findings.evidence: Header at schema.sql:9-12: 'Secondary INDEXES and CHECK constraints are NOT captured here - run pg_dump --schema-only if you need a byte-perfect copy.' Combined with the grep showing zero table CHECKs and only ~12 explicit indexes in the file, the canonical mirror is by its own admission incomplete for exactly the two areas this review targets.
findings.fixApproach: OWNER: run `pg_dump --schema-only` (or the existing supabase/diagnostics/export_live_schema.sql) and share. CLAUDE: reconcile CHECKs + indexes into schema.sql so the mirror is authoritative, and update the header disclaimer once captured.

findings.title: Handled API 500s never reach Sentry; no log drain → weak error observability
findings.problem: `Sentry.captureException`/`captureMessage` is called in only two places in the whole app (lib/rate-limit.ts and app/api/csp-report/route.ts). Sentry's `onRequestError` (instrumentation.ts:12, `captureRequestError`) only fires for UNHANDLED errors Next throws. But ~16 API route handlers catch their errors and return `NextResponse.json(..., {status:500})` (e.g. both cron routes do `console.error('[cron]', e)` then return 500). Because the error is caught, Next never sees a throw, so it never reaches Sentry — the failure exists only as a `console.error` in Vercel logs. No log drain is configured in the repo (Vercel Hobby log retention is short, no alerting).
findings.whyItMatters: Operational failures (DB errors, upload failures, cron failures, ticket-creation failures) are effectively invisible: they won't page anyone via Sentry and the short-lived Vercel logs that do hold them aren't drained or alerted. You can be silently failing in production.
findings.evidence: Repo-wide grep: Sentry.capture* appears only in lib/rate-limit.ts:82/84 and app/api/csp-report/route.ts:33. v3-snapshots L26-27 `catch (e) { console.error('[cron]', e); return NextResponse.json({ ok:false, error:'Snapshot failed' }, { status:500 }) }`.
findings.rootCause: Try/catch-and-return-500 pattern without an explicit Sentry.captureException in the catch blocks; reliance on onRequestError which only covers uncaught errors.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Every server-side 500 produces a captured Sentry event.
findings.fixApproach: Add `Sentry.captureException(e)` inside the catch blocks that return 500 (or a small `reportServerError()` helper used everywhere), and/or configure a Vercel log drain for retention + alerting.

findings.title: Archived-notification purge lives only in the unscheduled v3-recompute cron → notifications table grows unbounded
findings.problem: `vercel.json` schedules only one cron (`/api/cron/v3-snapshots` at 05:00). The repeat-defect recompute is safe (v3-snapshots line 17 already calls `runRepeatDefectRecompute()`), so `/api/cron/v3-recompute` is a redundant orphan — EXCEPT for one job unique to it: the archived-notification purge (v3-recompute lines 15-20, `notifications.delete()` where `archived_at` < now-3d). That delete is not in v3-snapshots and v3-recompute is never scheduled anywhere (no vercel.json entry, no GitHub Action, no inline caller — `runRepeatDefectRecompute` grep shows the route is its only consumer). So archived notifications are never purged.
findings.whyItMatters: On the Supabase free tier (500 MB) the `notifications` table grows without bound as completed-ticket notifications accumulate and are never deleted, slowly consuming the DB quota. Vercel Hobby allows 2 daily crons and only 1 is currently used, so there is a free slot — the purge simply isn't wired up.
findings.evidence: vercel.json crons = `[{ path: '/api/cron/v3-snapshots', schedule: '0 5 * * *' }]` only. v3-recompute L17-19 `createAdminClient().from('notifications').delete({count:'exact'}).not('archived_at','is',null).lt('archived_at', cutoff)` — this delete has no scheduled trigger.
findings.rootCause: Purge logic was placed in a cron route that was later dropped from vercel.json (or never added) while its recompute sibling got folded into v3-snapshots.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Old archived notifications are pruned on a schedule so the table does not grow unbounded.
findings.fixApproach: Fold the archived-notification purge delete into the already-scheduled v3-snapshots handler (cheapest, no extra cron slot), or add v3-recompute to vercel.json crons.
findings.scoreImpact: Slow free-tier DB exhaustion over time.

findings.title: xlsx (SheetJS) pinned to a CDN tarball is invisible to npm audit + Dependabot, and parses untrusted uploads
findings.problem: `xlsx` is pinned to `https://cdn.sheetjs.com/xlsx-0.20.3/xlsx-0.20.3.tgz` (package.json:39). Because it isn't a registry package, `npm audit` cannot see it (audit reports 0 xlsx advisories regardless) and Dependabot cannot bump it (explicitly noted in dependabot.yml — "manual quarterly check per C10"). The library is used to `XLSX.read()` user-supplied spreadsheet buffers in two routes (parse-quote-pdf:113 and projects import:46), i.e. it parses untrusted input. 0.20.3 does include the fixes for the known Prototype-Pollution (GHSA-4r6h-8v6p-xvw6) and ReDoS (GHSA-5pgg-2g8v-p4x9) advisories, so today it is patched — but any FUTURE xlsx advisory will not be surfaced by your CI audit or dependency bot.
findings.whyItMatters: A dependency that parses untrusted files while being excluded from both automated vulnerability scanning and automated patching is a standing supply-chain blind spot; the safety of the untrusted-spreadsheet parser then depends entirely on a human remembering the quarterly manual check.
findings.evidence: package.json:39 `"xlsx": "https://cdn.sheetjs.com/xlsx-0.20.3/xlsx-0.20.3.tgz"`; dependabot.yml comment: "`xlsx` is pinned to a SheetJS CDN tarball ... Dependabot can't bump it — manual quarterly check"; XLSX.read on user buffers at the two route lines above.
findings.rootCause: SheetJS moved distribution off the npm registry; the CDN pin is the correct remediation channel but breaks audit/Dependabot visibility.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: xlsx version is tracked against advisories despite being off-registry.
findings.fixApproach: Add an explicit scheduled reminder/check that compares the pinned xlsx version against SheetJS releases/advisories (e.g. a small CI step or calendared owner task), and keep the pin current. Confirm both parse routes are auth-gated and size-limited.
findings.scoreImpact: Supply-chain monitoring gap on an untrusted-input parser.

findings.title: Four moderate npm advisories via next's transitive postcss; non-blocking in CI
findings.problem: `npm audit` reports 4 moderate advisories, 0 high/critical: `postcss` (XSS via unescaped </style> in CSS stringify) reached transitively through `next@16.2.10`, plus `next`/`geist`/`@sentry/nextjs` flagged via that chain. The CI audit gate is `npm audit --omit=dev --audit-level=high` (ci.yml:77), so moderates do not fail the build. The postcss issue is build/CSS-toolchain only (needs attacker-controlled CSS run through postcss stringify), not a runtime request-path vuln for this app.
findings.whyItMatters: Low real-world exposure, but it is an accepted-open moderate set that will only clear when Next ships a patched postcss; worth tracking so it doesn't mask a future higher-severity moderate.
findings.evidence: `npm audit` summary: {moderate:4, high:0, critical:0}; chains: postcss←next, next←postcss, geist←next, @sentry/nextjs←next. ci.yml:77 `run: npm audit --omit=dev --audit-level=high`.
findings.rootCause: Transitive postcss version bundled by next 16.2.10.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Advisory count returns to zero after the upstream Next patch.
findings.fixApproach: Track the next release that bumps postcss and take the Dependabot bump when available; no code change needed now.

findings.title: Unauthenticated /api/csp-report forwards arbitrary POST bodies to Sentry at up to 60/min globally
findings.problem: `/api/csp-report` is intentionally unauthenticated (browsers send reports without a session) and rate-limited with a CONSTANT key `rateLimit('csp-report', 60, 60_000)` (line 17) — i.e. one shared 60/req-per-minute bucket for all callers, not per-IP. Each accepted request calls `Sentry.captureMessage(...)` with the caller-supplied report echoed into `extra: { report: r }` (lines 33-37). An attacker can POST crafted JSON and have it recorded in Sentry (and console.warn) at a sustained ~60/min = ~86k events/day, and because the bucket is global, a burst also suppresses legitimate CSP reports for everyone.
findings.whyItMatters: Not a data-exposure issue, but a cheap way to pollute Sentry / exhaust the free-tier event budget and to drown out real CSP violations. The global (non-per-client) limit both permits abuse volume and lets one noisy source starve genuine reports.
findings.evidence: csp-report/route.ts:17 `if (!(await rateLimit('csp-report', 60, 60_000))) return 429` (constant key); L36 `extra: { blocked, doc, report: r }` echoes attacker input into Sentry.
findings.rootCause: Constant rate-limit key + unauthenticated endpoint that writes user input to the error backend.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: CSP-report abuse cannot exhaust Sentry quota or suppress genuine reports.
findings.fixApproach: Key the rate limit per client IP (and/or lower the global cap well under the Sentry budget), and avoid stuffing the raw report object into Sentry `extra` (log only the whitelisted directive/blocked/doc fields).

findings.title: Executive role (documented read-only) is granted ticket write actions across its company
findings.lines: workflow TRANSITIONS include 'executive' on many actions (89-176); assign 36; quote-decision 39
findings.problem: CLAUDE.md describes 'executive' as an estate-wide READ-ONLY role, but the workflow role arrays and the assign/quote-decision/transition routes permit executive to perform state-changing actions (assign suppliers, approve/decline quotes, validate, approve/close-out) for any ticket in their company.
findings.whyItMatters: This is company-scoped (every path still checks ticket.company_id === prof.company_id), so it is not a cross-tenant hole, but it contradicts the documented read-only contract and widens the executive write surface. Worth confirming it is intentional; if execs are meant to be read-only, these are unintended write privileges.
findings.rootCause: Role capability drift between docs and the workflow transition matrix.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Executive capabilities match the intended contract (either genuinely read-only, or docs updated to reflect the write actions they are allowed).
findings.fixApproach: Decide the intended executive capability; either remove 'executive' from the write transition/role arrays and the assign/quote-decision role checks, or update the documentation to reflect intentional company-scoped write access.
testsRequired: Executive attempts assign/approve_quote → behavior matches the agreed contract
findings.scoreImpact: Role over-privilege vs documented read-only intent (company-scoped).

findings.title: sla_rules read policy exposes global (company_id IS NULL) rows to anonymous users
findings.problem: `create policy "sla read" on public.sla_rules for select using (((company_id IS NULL) OR (company_id = app_company_id())));` (schema.sql:1595-1597). Combined with `grant select on all tables in schema public to anon` (grant file:25), the `company_id IS NULL` branch is true for anon (app_company_id() returns null for them), so anonymous/unauthenticated callers can read the global default SLA-rule rows.
findings.whyItMatters: Minor information disclosure — the platform's default SLA thresholds are readable without auth. No PII or tenant data, so info-level.
findings.evidence: schema.sql:1597 `using (((company_id IS NULL) OR (company_id = app_company_id())))`; grant file:25 anon SELECT.
findings.rootCause: The `company_id IS NULL` convenience branch is unconditional and evaluates true for the anon context.
findings.requiredSecureOutcome: Default SLA rules should require an authenticated session at minimum.
findings.fixApproach: Add `auth.role() = 'authenticated'` (or `auth.uid() IS NOT NULL`) to the NULL branch of the sla read policy.
testsRequired: Anon SELECT on sla_rules -> 0 rows

findings.title: Deny-all tables and SECURITY DEFINER functions reviewed — no defects found (confirmation)
findings.problem: CONFIRMATION (not a defect).
